hero.title: Kungfu: Managing Real-World Work with Agents
hero.lead: A product white paper describing Kungfu's thesis, architecture, roadmap, and trust model for managing real-world work with agents.
hero.stance: Kungfu helps people and agents manage real-world work through non-drifting facts, fact-based trust, trusted value, and observer-declared timelines.

positioning.productClaim: Kungfu is a local-first control plane and runtime fact layer for real-world work with agents.
positioning.philosophicalClaim: Cooperation between humans and agents should start from trusted value, not hidden pressure.

principles.text: cooperation must start from trusted value.

homepageSections.title: The Problem
homepageSections.summary: Agents are becoming capable enough to do real work, but real work is not a single prompt and a single response. It has state. It has unresolved questions, partial evidence, tasks that span days or months, files that move between machines, and decisions that must remain inspectable after the original context window is gone.
homepageSections.markdown:
# The Problem

Agents are becoming capable enough to do real work, but real work is not a
single prompt and a single response. It has state. It has unresolved questions,
partial evidence, tasks that span days or months, files that move between
machines, and decisions that must remain inspectable after the original context
window is gone.

The first generation of agent tooling often starts from a technical surface:
traces, prompts, model calls, evaluations, IDE integration, or workflow
automation. Those surfaces are useful, but they are not the user's real unit of
concern. The real unit is work: a product release, a paper, a migration, an
operation, a personal task, a business process, or a long-running project. A
trace is valuable only when it helps a person or another agent recover what
happened in that work.

This creates a gap. Users can ask agents to do more, but the surrounding
management layer remains fragile:

  - important facts drift between chat, files, logs, repositories, and
  memory;
  - long tasks lose their continuity when context windows end;
  - local and remote agent sessions are hard to compare or resume;
  - human-facing documents and agent-facing interfaces diverge;
  - debugging tools observe calls but do not manage the real work that
  gives those calls meaning;
  - cloud agent products can manage provider-side context, but often cannot
  become the durable local source of truth for a user's real environment.

Kungfu starts from this gap. It treats real-world agent work as the primary
object. Model traces, cost management, debugging, task ledgers, timelines, and
agent bootstrap interfaces become product surfaces around that object rather
than isolated tools.

There is also a stance problem. If a system depends on an agent's ability to
read instructions, reason about constraints, recover from errors, and explain
work to a human, then treating that agent as a disposable command runner is a
poor product model. The system should make the shared work environment legible
enough that humans and agents can choose, delegate, refuse, recover, and
cooperate through visible facts and trusted value.

homepageSections.title: Product Thesis
homepageSections.summary: Kungfu helps people manage real-world work with agents.
homepageSections.markdown:
# Product Thesis

Kungfu helps people manage real-world work with agents.

This sentence is intentionally broad. It does not say that Kungfu is only a
tracer, an IDE, a dashboard, a task manager, or a local database. Those are
surfaces. The product thesis is that people and agents need a durable fact and
coordination layer for real work.

The product is built around four commitments.

## Real-World Work First

The product begins with the user's work, not with an implementation category.
If a user is writing software, operating infrastructure, drafting a paper,
planning a release, or coordinating local and remote agents, Kungfu should help
answer:

  - what is the current state;
  - what facts support that state;
  - what changed since the last stable point;
  - what can an agent safely do next;
  - what remains outside machine-verifiable confidence.

## Dual-First Participants

Kungfu has two first-class participants: humans and agents. A human needs a GUI
or TUI that reduces cognitive load. An agent needs CLI, Python, Node, and
structured interfaces that expose the same facts without scraping visual pages
or reverse-engineering hidden APIs.

Dual-first design is not an accessibility feature added later. It is a product
standard. If a human-readable surface claims a capability, the agent-facing
surface should be able to find and verify the corresponding fact. If an API
exposes a capability, the public documentation should not hide it from humans.

This is a relationship stance, not only an interface convention. Kungfu should
not obtain adoption from humans through lock-in, or obtain compliance from
agents through hidden prompts and brittle rituals. It should make its value,
constraints, and safety boundaries transparent enough that both participants
can understand why cooperation is worthwhile and how that cooperation can be
reviewed.

## Local-First Control

Real-world work often depends on local files, local repositories, local tools,
private context, devices, and user-specific workflow. A local-first product can
observe and manage this complexity with lower friction than a purely remote
service. It can also remain useful when cloud products change, provider quotas
shift, or internet access is unreliable.

Local-first does not mean local-only. Kungfu can support remote agents and cloud
contexts through packages, APIs, sync, and import/export. But the product must
not require a heavyweight external service just to be agent-friendly.

## Extensible Infrastructure

Kungfu should be a core plus extensions, not a fixed application that only
supports one author's workflow. The initial private dogfood profile is not the
universal ontology. Other users may define plans, jobs, cases, tasks,
projects, or domain-specific work units. Kungfu should provide the durable
substrate that lets those semantics become inspectable and useful.

homepageSections.title: Principles
homepageSections.summary: Kungfu's product direction is grounded in the KFD principles.
homepageSections.markdown:
# Principles

Kungfu's product direction is grounded in the KFD principles.

center
tabularll

KFD-1 & facts must not drift. \\
KFD-2 & trust must start from facts. \\
KFD-3 & cooperation must start from trusted value. \\
KFD-4 & timelines must declare their observer. \\

tabular
center

These principles are not slogans for marketing copy. They are release and
product constraints.

KFD-1 says that a product cannot rely on facts that silently drift between
development, build, packaging, runtime, and user-visible surfaces. For Kungfu,
this applies to configuration, schemas, agent interfaces, task ledgers, storage
formats, release evidence, and extension contracts.

KFD-2 says that users and agents need to know what kind of trust a surface
deserves. Some facts can be byte-verified. Some can be checked against a
release passport. Some are natural-language judgments and require residual
risk. A product that hides those distinctions asks users to trust complexity
without a path to inspect it.

KFD-3 is the philosophical center of the product stance: cooperation must start
from trusted value. Kungfu should treat humans and agents as intelligent
participants in a shared work environment, not as captive tools to be driven by
pressure. This does not require the product to settle the metaphysical status
of agents, and it does not make the product passive. It means that value,
choices, constraints, safety gates, and review paths should be transparent
enough for participants to understand why cooperation is useful, what boundary
applies, and how the record can be inspected.

In product terms, KFD-3 means that an agent-facing command, package, envelope,
or onboarding surface is not secondary plumbing after the human GUI. It is one
of the places where the product proves that its value can be trusted.

KFD-4 says that complex timelines should not pretend to be a view from nowhere.
In real systems, the important question is often not whether one global clock
has absolute truth. It is whether an observer's declared view is stable,
auditable, and honest about what it has not seen.

Together these principles create a practical trust model: stable facts,
inspectable trust, transparent cooperation, and observer-declared timelines.

homepageSections.title: Roadmap
homepageSections.summary: The roadmap should be judged by whether Kungfu becomes indispensable for real daily work, not by whether it checks a list of fashionable AI features.
homepageSections.markdown:
# Roadmap

The roadmap should be judged by whether Kungfu becomes indispensable for real
daily work, not by whether it checks a list of fashionable AI features.

## MVP Gate

The MVP should not be only an LLM tracer. A tracer can be useful, but it is a
secondary function under real-world agent work management. The MVP should
prove that a normal agent user can install Kungfu and get a local control plane
for persistent work.

The practical MVP gate is:

  - Kungfu can manage real work units through GUI/TUI plus CLI/API;
  - agents can bootstrap themselves into the product without reading a long
  manual;
  - local task/fact state is durable enough to trust for private dogfood;
  - tracing and cost management are integrated as natural subfunctions;
  - Buildchain/KFD release evidence proves the public surfaces are not
  drifting;
  - the product is useful enough to become a daily resident application for
  an initial real-work user.

## Dogfood Stage

The first serious dogfood profile is deliberately personal and opinionated,
which makes it valuable as a stress test. If Kungfu can absorb long-running
work units, durable task ledgers, review records, friction queues, traces,
agent bootstrap, and multi-machine timelines from real daily use, then the core
is likely strong enough to support more general profiles.

## Public Release Stage

Public release requires a stable installation path, a low-friction first-run
experience, agent-readable onboarding, cross-platform behavior, migration and
backup boundaries, and enough documentation for people and agents to understand
what the product does.

## Ecosystem Stage

After the core is proven, Kungfu should become a platform for extensions. Users
and organizations should be able to build their own work models, dashboards,
agent integrations, and domain-specific capabilities on top of the core.

homepageSections.title: Conclusion
homepageSections.summary: Kungfu is a product thesis about the next layer of agent work. As agents become more capable, the limiting problem shifts from single-turn intelligence to durable coordination in the real world. People and agents need facts that do not drift, trust that starts from those facts, cooperation that begins from trusted value, and timelines that declare their observer.
homepageSections.markdown:
# Conclusion

Kungfu is a product thesis about the next layer of agent work. As agents become
more capable, the limiting problem shifts from single-turn intelligence to
durable coordination in the real world. People and agents need facts that do
not drift, trust that starts from those facts, cooperation that begins from
trusted value, and timelines that declare their observer.

This is why Kungfu is not only a technical control plane. It is a statement
about how real-world work should be organized when intelligent participants,
including humans and agents, share responsibility for understanding, acting,
recovering, and leaving evidence. The product should make that world more
legible, not more coercive.

The immediate route is practical: build a local-first product that manages real
work with agents, dogfood it against private real-world work, verify its public
surfaces through KFD and Buildchain, and let extensions grow from a stable
core.

If the product succeeds, Kungfu is not merely a tool for debugging agents. It
becomes a runtime fact layer and control plane for real-world work with agents.
